heading: CXVIII SAVERIO CAMPANINI
card_summary: This chunk constitutes the concluding section of Campanini's scholarly introduction to the Bompiani edition of De Harmonia Mundi, cataloguing Zorzi's extensive European Fortleben from the sixteenth through seventeenth centuries. Campanini names direct influenced figures including Giulio Camillo Delminio, Agrippa's De occulta philosophia, Guillaume Postel, Robert Fludd, the Cambridge Platonists (Henry More), the Pléiade poets (especially Pontus de Tyard), Guy Le Fèvre de la Boderie's French translation, and poets John Donne and John Milton. As a capstone, Campanini translates in full the closing hymn of the De Harmonia Mundi — the final 'accordo' of the final 'modulo' of the final 'tono' of the third canticle — a mystical apophatic prayer addressed to the ineffable God, dense with kabbalistic, Neoplatonic, and scriptural imagery. The chunk closes with Campanini's acknowledgments and a Latin Arabic proverb quoted from Zorzi.
essay:
## Overview

Chunk 26 forms the close of Campanini's substantial scholarly introduction to his Italian edition and translation of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. It has two distinct parts: first, a survey of Zorzi's European reception (*Fortleben*) from the sixteenth century onward; second, a complete Italian rendering of the work's closing mystical hymn, which Campanini presents as the spiritual heart of the entire edifice.

---

## Zorzi's European Reception (Fortleben)

Campanini identifies the mapping of Zorzi's influence as an urgent but still incomplete scholarly task. He names a remarkable breadth of figures touched by Zorzi's Christian Kabbalah and harmonic cosmology:

- **Giulio Camillo Delminio** — the architect of the famous Memory Theatre
- **Marcello Palingenio Stellato** — author of the *Zodiacus Vitae* (printed by Bernardino Vitali, probably 1536)
- **Achille Bocchi** and **Ulisse Aldrovandi**
- **Tommaso Garzoni da Bagnacavallo**, whose *Piazza universale* is said to abound in Zorzian citations
- **Giambattista Marino** and **Guillaume Postel**
- **Robert Fludd** and **Henry More** and the **Cambridge Platonists**
- **Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa von Nettesheim**, whose *De occulta philosophia* contains citations from Zorzi bordering on plagiarism
- **Jacques Gaffarel**, who offered an impassioned defense of Zorzi
- **John Donne** and **John Milton** (with the caveat that Milton's dependence on genuinely kabbalistic sources has been contested)

Campanini further highlights the profound affinity between Zorzi and **Leone Ebreo**'s *Dialoghi d'amore* as a relationship still insufficiently studied.

The French reception receives particular emphasis. Zorzi's works were repeatedly reprinted in France in the second half of the sixteenth century, and the *De Harmonia Mundi* was translated into French by **Guy Le Fèvre de la Boderie**. Campanini cites J.-F. Maillard's claim that the work belongs by rights to the history of French literature. The Pléiade poets, especially **Pontus de Tyard**, were durably influenced by the Venetian friar.

An unrealized plan by the Venetian **Accademia della Fama** to translate the Latin text into Italian is also noted as part of the work's reception history.

> *"il De harmonia mundi, lo si è ricordato, fu tradotto in francese da Guy Le Fèvre de la Boderie. Il prestigio e l'influsso dell'opera di Zorzi in Francia furono tali che J.-F. Maillard... è arrivato ad asserire che il De harmonia mundi appartiene di diritto alla storia della letteratura francese."*

<cite>p. 118–119</cite>

Campanini concludes the survey by calling for a systematic reconstruction of the debts Zorzi's posterity contracted toward his peculiar vision of Christian Kabbalah — one that made Zorzi himself, as an avid collector of Hebrew and non-Hebrew sources, into a fountainhead, not always acknowledged, of European thought and literature.

---

## The Closing Hymn of *De Harmonia Mundi*

To emphasize the essentially mystical character of the work, Campanini cedes the floor to Zorzi himself and presents in full the final 'accordo' (*agreement/chord*) of the final 'modulo' (*module*) of the final 'tono' (*tone*) of the third *cantico* — the structural terminus of the entire *harmonia*. Campanini describes its density of thought as very high, while noting that the inspiring center remains visible: an inexhaustible meditation on the unity of cosmos, Scripture, and humanity, whose grammar is marked by a vertiginous succession of images held apart from collapsed equivalences by the distinctly kabbalistic force of symbolic separation and union.

The hymn is titled:

> *"Inno da cantare in silenzio soltanto per Dio, con mente pura e intenta verso l'alto"*

<cite>p. 119</cite>

### Apophatic Theology and Divine Attributes

The hymn opens in a mode of classical apophatic theology. God is addressed as immense, eternal, and ineffable (*immenso, eterno e ineffabile*), uncircumscribed by any reasoning, inaccessible, containing all things and yet remote from all. Human intellect can speak or think of God only insofar as His light has illuminated it.

> *"Tu non sei limitato da alcun termine, non sei circoscritto da nessun ragionamento, l'accesso a Te non è facile per nessuno, Tu che contieni tutte le cose e sei la causa del tutto; benché Tu esista in tutte le cose, sei da tutte così remoto che di Te la mente umana non può dire né pensare se non in quanto la Tua luce l'ha illuminata."*

<cite>p. 119–120</cite>

### Trinitarian and Kabbalistic Imagery

God is called "Padre di tutto, luce pura e inaccessibile, sorgente profonda, creatore nascosto" — the Father of all, pure and inaccessible light, deep source, hidden creator. The Trinitarian structure surfaces: only the Son knows the Father, and only he to whom the Son wills to reveal Him. God is identified with the eternal Word (*Verbo eterno*) that gives all things.

The passage then accumulates a dense series of divine titles with strong kabbalistic resonance: God is the hidden dwelling (*dimora nascosta*), being, life, foundation, principle and end of all; He is described as the *sigillo* (seal), *forma* (form), *spirito* (spirit), *legame* (bond), and *vita del mondo* (life of the world). These terms map onto the *sefirot* of the kabbalistic Tree, particularly onto *Keter*, *Chokhmah*, *Binah*, and *Tiferet*.

> *"Tu sei la dimora nascosta, l'essere, la vita, il fondamento, il principio e il termine di tutto; Tu santo, benedetto, Tu sei Dio altissimo, il sigillo, la forma, lo spirito, il legame e la vita del mondo."*

<cite>p. 120</cite>

God is also called the *albero della conoscenza* (tree of knowledge), the *sorgente delle anime e delle forme* (source of souls and forms), and the *pace di tutto, l'unità e il vincolo* (peace of all, unity and bond) — again registers mixing Neoplatonic emanationism with kabbalistic nomenclature.

### The Secret Manna and the Imperative of Silence

The hymn culminates in a remarkable passage on the impossibility and impropriety of speaking the mystery at all. The secret is compared to the *manna nascosta* (hidden manna) of Revelation 2:17, which only the one who receives it knows. Spiritual understanding does not come through hearing but through the eternal light. Words must be hidden in the heart. The King's mystery must be concealed; it is literally ineffable and cannot be divulged.

> *"Chi è sapiente comprenda questo, perché si tratta della manna segreta, che nessuno conosce se non chi la riceve, perciò non è possibile parlarne."*

<cite>p. 121</cite>

The hymn closes with the injunction to praise God in silence, citing Isaiah (the scriptural warrant for "in silence and hope shall be our strength") and Jeremiah ("it is good to wait in silence"), and looking forward to the beatific vision when the tongue will be loosed for proper praise, citing Psalm 84 ("those who dwell in your house will praise you forever and ever"). The final word is *Amen*.

---

## Arabic Proverb and Acknowledgments

Campanini appends, before the acknowledgments, a Latin Arabic proverb attributed directly to Francesco Zorzi:

> *"Apud Arabes est pulcherrimum adagium: Bonum operare, quod potes, et illud in aquam proiice, quia piscis aliquis educet ipsum aliquando foras in tui beneficium."*

<cite>p. 122</cite>

This proverb — do good as you can and cast it into the water, for some fish will draw it out some day for your benefit — stands as a final motto for the entire scholarly endeavor.

The acknowledgments name Campanini's principal intellectual debts: Giulio Busi (initiator and tireless spur to the project), Oxford mentors Brad Sabin Hill and Daniel Frank, Mauro Perani, Wilhelm Schmidt-Biggemann (Freie Universität Berlin), Giovanni Reale, and financial support from the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG). The volume closes with a German poetic exchange: a quotation from a poet, addressing the wife Katharina.

---

## Significance for Zorzi's Cosmological Project

The closing hymn is structurally and theologically decisive: the entire tripartite architecture of the *De Harmonia Mundi* — with its tones, modules, and accordi — resolves into an act of mystical silence. Kabbalistic symbol, Neoplatonic light-metaphysics, Trinitarian theology, and scriptural citation fuse into a single apophatic gesture. The harmony of the world, having been traced through all its numerical, musical, and cosmological registers, ends not in a triumphant declaration but in an injunction to silence before the ineffable. This confirms Campanini's thesis that the work is fundamentally mystical in character, not merely encyclopedic or philosophical.
